title: Stair Baluster Outdoor Console Table
summary: A slatted console table with a lower notched shelf, standing on square stair-baluster legs.
description:
Stair balusters make surprisingly good table legs — they come pre-milled square and straight, which saves you the trouble of squaring up your own leg stock. The table stands on a two-tier apron frame: a top tier that carries the slatted tabletop, and a lower tier that carries a second slatted shelf, notched around each leg so the shelf sits flush rather than floating above them.

Both the top and the shelf are built from short slats rather than solid boards, clamped tight to each rail as you attach them — clamping each joint, not just the first one, is what keeps a multi-slat surface like this from creeping out of flat by the time you reach the last piece. Use exterior glue throughout since this table lives outside.

tools.note: Cuts the corner notches in the shelf rails so they fit around the legs.
Clamp every slat joint, not just the first, to keep the top and shelf flat.

cutList.note: Notched at each end to fit around the legs.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: From the 1x4 cut ten top slats and nine shelf slats at 9", two top edge rails at 39-1/2", two top aprons at 33" and two at 11-1/2". From the 1x3 cut two shelf edge rails at 35-1/2", two bottom aprons at 33" and two at 11-1/2". The four stair balusters are used at their full 34" length as the legs — no cut needed, which is the whole reason for using them. Cut the nineteen 9" slats against a stop block so they come out identical; slats that vary by even a sixteenth show as uneven gaps across the finished top.

steps.title: Build the tabletop
steps.body: Lay the two 39-1/2" top edge rails flat and parallel with a 9" gap between their inside faces, and fill that gap with the ten 9" slats running front to back. Ten 3-1/2" slats across a 39-1/2" rail leaves 4-1/2" to share, so a 1/2" spacer between slats lands the end slats flush with the rail ends and the finished top at 39-1/2" x 16". Join each slat to each rail with exterior glue and 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws driven from underneath, and clamp every joint as you make it — not just the first one — which is what keeps a multi-slat top flat instead of creeping out of true by the last piece.

steps.title: Notch the shelf rails and build the shelf
steps.body: Before assembling anything, cut a 1-1/4" square notch in each end of both 35-1/2" shelf edge rails with the jigsaw so the shelf drops around the legs and sits flush in the lower frame rather than floating above it. Mark each notch off an actual baluster instead of off the number, and cut just outside the line so you can pare it to a snug fit. Then build the shelf like the top: the two notched rails parallel with a 9" gap, the nine 9" slats between them, and the 4" of slack across a 35-1/2" rail spread as 1/2" gaps. Glue and screw as before, clamping each joint.

steps.title: Build the leg and apron frame
steps.body: Drill 3/4" pocket holes in both ends of all eight aprons first. Build one end assembly: an 11-1/2" top apron between two balusters with its top edge flush with the tops of the legs, and an 11-1/2" bottom apron set at whatever shelf height you want — the plan does not specify one, so pick it and mark both ends the same. Build the second end against the first so the two match, then join them with the 33" long aprons top and bottom, checking square with the speed square and by comparing diagonals. Also drill a row of pocket holes along the inside top edge of both top aprons, pointing up, for fastening the tabletop next — reaching them after the top is on is impossible.

steps.title: Attach the top and shelf, then sand
steps.body: Set the tabletop upside down, center the leg frame on it — the frame is 35-1/2" x 14" under a 39-1/2" x 16" top, so you are looking for 2" of overhang at each end and 1" front and back — and drive 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws up through the top aprons into the slats and rails. Turn it upright and drop the notched shelf into the lower frame; the notches should let it settle onto the bottom aprons with the rails tight against the legs, then glue and screw it down. Stand the table on a flat surface and check all four balusters touch before the glue sets. Sand every surface and ease the slat edges and corners — this piece lives outside and gets handled.

images.alt: Outdoor console table with stair-baluster legs and a slatted top and shelf
